data.title: Energy Transfer: 2 Ways To Play This, With An Obvious Winner
data.description:
Those who follow my work know that I have a hyper-concentrated portfolio. I firmly believe that your 10th-best idea probably is not that great. One of the hold things in my portfolio is midstream/pipeline enterprise Energy Transfer LP (ET). On a personal level, I own a lot of it. In fact, right now, it is my second-largest holding. In total, approximately 14.2% of all of my assets are tied up in the company. However, there are more ways than one to invest in the enterprise. Personally, I own the common units. There are a couple of reasons for this. The big one is that I get greater upside. But for those who prefer, there is the opportunity to acquire the Series I Fixed Rate Perpetual Referred Units, Energy Transfer LP 9.250% FXD PFD I (ET.PR.I). These pay out pretty hefty distributions themselves, and there are legitimate reasons to prefer those over common units.

In my last article about Energy Transfer, my emphasis was on the common shares of the company. But to the best of my recollection, I have not written about the preferred stock in any capacity, and all the articles that I have written are about the enterprise. I figured we are long past due for such a deep dive. And what I found was that, while I can understand these being a preferred method for some investors, my overall view is that, instead of deserving a ‘strong buy’ rating like Energy Transfer does, the preferred units deserve nothing more than a solid ‘buy’.

When preferred is not ideal

In case you are new to Energy Transfer, the company operates as a massive midstream/pipeline operator. It has over 140,000 miles of pipeline to its name. In my previous article, I dug in deeper than just that. But the bottom line is that its network of assets is vast and growing. In 2025 alone, the company earmarked $4.6 billion for growth opportunities. That was actually down from the previous expectation of $5 billion, primarily because of weaker financial performance achieved in the third quarter of 2025.

To be honest with you, I believe that the US economy is heading toward a recession. I have been saying as much since August of 2025. Pipeline companies tend to get hit during downturns anyway. But historically speaking, they are fundamentally solid. This is because they don't make much from the price of the products going through their pipelines. Rather, their business is centered around volume and asset spreads. Still, in the event that I am correct, it's not unthinkable that shares of Energy Transfer (the common units) could fall. Already, units are down 16.3% over the last year. And while the hefty yield of 8.02% helps to cushion that fall, it only goes so far towards soothing the burns.

Historically speaking, Energy Transfer has had an alternative way for investors to buy in. Since 2023, they have had their Series I Preferred Units listed on the New York Stock Exchange. Back in November of 2023, these units were issued in connection with the company's purchase of Crestwood, a business that focused on gathering and processing assets in the Williston, Delaware, and Powder River basins. Shareholders of that company received 2.07 common shares of Energy Transfer at that time. And holders of preferred units in Crestwood will ultimately be given about 41.5 million shares of preferred stock, as well as $300 million of cash. In total, this was a pretty decent-sized acquisition, costing about $3.65 billion in total.

As the table above illustrates, there are other series of preferred stock that have as well. But all of these others are privately owned. This means you cannot buy them on the open market. When it comes to the overall cap table of Energy Transfer, the publicly traded preferred units are relatively small. In the same table, you can see the composition of preferred units that the company has had on its books over the last few years now. Overall, preferred exposure has dropped. However, even amongst the preferred units, the Series I Preferred Unit accounts for only a small portion of the overall pie. We are talking about $378.5 million in total. For a company with an enterprise value of around $133.17 billion, that is a rounding error.

For investors who want both stability and cash flow, this often-overlooked opportunity can be appealing. And in fact, many investors have latched on to this. The reason why I can say that is because, based on the terms of the units, their liquidation preference is only $9.1273. But right now, they are trading at $11.40. The fixed payout of 9.25% of their liquidation preference and the seniority that they all offer above the common units justify a premium and translate to an effective yield at this moment of 7.41%. That is below the 8.04% yield that shareholders of the common stock currently have. But there are reasons for that disparity.

As I mentioned already, the preferred units are senior to the common units. This is a universal truth in the world of preferred stock. This means that, upon a sale, liquidation, bankruptcy, or some other major corporate event, the preferred shareholders get paid first. The probability of a company such as Energy Transfer going under is exceedingly small and would likely require something like fraud to take place. But this additional degree of safety makes them a safe haven that happens to offer a yield that is essentially comparable to the common units. This also means that they tend to be less volatile. Historically speaking, preferred units move up and down a lot less than common units do. So even in the event of an economic downturn, investors can essentially have their cake and eat it too.

The problem with buying the preferred units is that the upside is capped. They are already trading at a premium to their liquidation preference. Further upside on principle is limited, meaning that these should be treated more like a perpetual bond than a stock. And over the long run, we should expect these preferred units to underperform the common, perhaps substantially so. In the chart below, you can see certain financial metrics that Energy Transfer has achieved over the last few years. Although some financial performance has been lumpy, the overall trend here has been positive. The company is a cash cow that continues to grow. And what's really great here is that the preferred units cost almost nothing. In the first nine months of 2025, they cost the business only $198 million in distributions. That compares to the $3.50 billion paid out to common shareholders.

Another thing to consider here is that the risk profile of the preferred units does change as the balance sheet of the company changes. Even though the preferred units rank senior to common units, they are subordinate to all debt. As the chart below illustrates, overall net debt for Energy Transfer has changed over time. It has increased, in fact. But ever since the net leverage ratio peaked at 3.94 in 2023, it has been declining. It stands at 3.72 right now.

In the chart below, you can see how this stacks up against the net leverage ratio of five other similar enterprises. At the moment, only one of these firms has less leverage than our candidate does. So the good news is that, even though net debt has increased, the risk profile of the company is low enough that it is unlikely that preferred shareholders will miss out on any capital in the long haul. Absent a Black Swan event or years of mismanagement, these are a stable source of upside.

Normally, I would take this opportunity to value Energy Transfer. But doing so would focus more on the common units than the preferred units. In my last article about the enterprise, published late last year, I did provide such an analysis. What I found at that time was that, in addition to the stock trading on the cheap, with a price-to-adjusted operating cash flow multiple of only 4.9 and an EV-to-EBITDA multiple of 8.3, it was trading lower than any of the five institutions that I compared it to. I quantified upside at that time between 67.3% and 157.8%, with this upside based solely on the assumption that the firm should trade at multiples more comparable with those of these competitors. But since then, the stock has barely budged, with investors achieving upside of only 0.1%. So it would be redundant to go through that whole process again. What that does indicate, however, is that while buying the preferred units can be a great way to capture stability, safety, and cash flows that are all but guaranteed, taking just slightly more risk by buying the common stock provides not only a slightly higher yield today, but it also offers significantly more upside should things go right.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I believe that while Energy Transfer’s common units represent one of the best opportunities out there right now, the preferred units are less than stellar. They certainly aren't bad. And I would argue that, in some respects, they can be attractive for the right investor. But for anybody looking to materially outperform the market in the long run, accepting the slightly higher risk and the greater volatility and going for the common units makes more sense. Still, because of the overall value proposition, I believe that the preferred units deserve a ‘buy’ rating at this time.

data.title: Conestoga Small Cap Composite Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Small Cap Composite Net Performance (as of 12/31/25)*

Small Cap Composite – 4Q25 Performance & Attribution

The Conestoga Small Cap Composite posted a total return of -1.89% net-of-fees in the fourth quarter of 2025, underperforming the Russell 2000 Growth Index return of 1.22%. Market returns were volatile in the quarter, with the first two weeks seeing a continuation of the sharp rally from the early April lows until concerns over rising credit risks triggered a sudden 10% correction over the next six weeks, before ultimately finishing the quarter strong on back of interest rate cuts and robust third quarter earnings reports. From a macro perspective, we noted in the quarter that while “low quality” stocks again outperformed, the degree of outperformance was more muted than it had been earlier in the year. After trailing by more than 23% across 2Q and 3Q, profitable stocks in the Russell 2000 Growth Index lagged unprofitable ones by just 8% in 4Q. More importantly, once the "low quality" rally peaked on October 15th, profitable stocks in the Russell 2000 Growth Index bested unprofitable ones by more than 5% through year-end 2025, and our strategy outperformed by more than 2% over the same period.

While our returns benefited in the fourth quarter from the pullback in the most speculative winners of the April to October rally, these gains were offset by negative sector allocation and stock selection in the Health Care sector. Specifically, Small Cap biotechnology and pharmaceuticals stocks, where we are materially underweight the Index, represented 132% of the Russell 2000 Growth's total returns in the fourth quarter, versus just 11% year-to-date through the end of the third quarter. Four of our six Health Care stocks were positive contributors to performance in the fourth quarter, led by Repligen Corp. (RGEN) and Vericel Corp. (VCEL), while Stevanato Group SpA (STVN) and LeMaitre Vascular, Inc. (LMAT) were the detractors. The challenge was keeping up with the overall Health Care sector’s 18% overall returns, led by biotechnology and pharmaceuticals’ gains of 28% and 19%, respectively.

Returns were also challenging in the Industrials sector, where six of our sixteen positions added to returns and our more cyclically exposed holdings struggled. AAON, Inc. (AAON) and Construction Partners, Inc. (ROAD) lagged after reporting weaker than expected 3Q25 results, while Trex Co., Inc. (TREX) continued to suffer from competitive pressures and the slower and longer-lasting housing and remodeling environment.

On the positive side, stock selection was particularly strong in Financials and Basic Materials, while sector allocation drove gains in Consumer Discretionary. Within Financials, Clearwater Analytics Holdings, Inc. (CWAN) outperformed dramatically on back of a mid-quarter announcement that the company would be acquired. In Basic Materials, RBC Bearings, Inc. (RBC) was a standout thanks to strong tailwinds in their aerospace and defense businesses, as well as robust operating leverage, margin expansion, and free cash flow conversion. Finally, our gains in Consumer Discretionary were driven by a combination of our relatively low weight in the sector, on top of positive relative performance in our two holdings.

Small Cap Composite – Full Year 2025 Performance & Attribution

Moving to the full year, the Composite outperformed the Russell 2000 Growth Index through early May, when the U.S. and China agreed to temporarily halt the newly introduced tariffs and begin negotiations on a broader deal, further accelerating the rally, which had already taken the Index up over 20% since April 8th. And then, once that rally peaked on October 15th, the Composite again outperformed the Russell 2000 Growth Index through year-end. In fact, the entirety of our Small Cap Composite’s relative performance challenges came during the April 8th-October 15th period when the Russell 2000 Growth Index rallied over 49% and was led higher by a historically narrow subset of stocks with unprofitable, high beta, and long duration characteristics. In total, our Composite trailed the Russell 2000 Growth Index in 2025 with a return of -10.13% net of fees versus the benchmark’s return of 13.01%.

While challenging from a relative performance perspective, sharp “low quality” rallies like we experienced in 2025 historically have taken place at the very beginning of new Small Cap bull markets. These “low quality” rallies tend to last roughly a full year but see their largest gains in the first six months before trailing off and finally ceding leadership back to “high quality” over the next six months. That historical precedent is important here because while unprofitable stocks in the Russell 2000 Growth Index outperformed their profitable peers by more than 58% in the roughly six months between April 8th and October 15th market rally, they then lagged by -5% from mid-October through year-end, providing hope that while it could be choppy for a few more months, the worst of the “low quality” headwind may be behind us.

In terms of full year performance effects, stock selection was most challenging in Technology, where fears of the “AI eating Software” theme gripped the market and caused significant multiple contraction across the group, with SPS Commerce, Inc. (SPSC), Vertex, Inc. (VERX), and Descartes Systems Group, Inc. (DSGX) experiencing the largest negative effects in our portfolio.

Within Industrials, 9 of our 22 stocks were positive contributors to performance, while weakness in three of our holdings had a particularly adverse impact on overall results. AAON, Inc. (AAON) suffered from a slowdown in demand for HVAC equipment following several years of strong growth. Trex Co., Inc. (TREX), as mentioned earlier, struggled in the face of competitive pressures and an extended slowdown in the residential repair and remodel market, while Transcat, Inc. (TRNS) saw persistent organic service revenue weakness and margin compression following a series of acquisitions which weighed on profitability and investor sentiment.

Finally, our sector underweight in Health Care – and specifically our minimal exposure to the Small Cap biotechnology and pharmaceuticals industries – hindered relative returns as these two industries were among the best performing industries in the Russell 2000 Growth Index in 2025. One bright spot was solid gains in our high conviction position in Repligen Corp. (RGEN), but it was outweighed by weakness in Vericel Corp. (VCEL), Neogen Corp. (NGEN), and Azenta, Inc. (AZTA).

Small Cap Composite – 4Q25 Top 5 Leaders

Clearwater Analytics Holdings, Inc.

CWAN provides cloud-based investment accounting and analytics software to institutional investors and insurers. Shares significantly outperformed in 4Q25 following the announcement that the company would be acquired, which drove a re-rating toward the agreed transaction value. The acquisition highlighted the strategic value of CWAN’s highly recurring revenue model, strong client retention, and critical role within institutional investment operations.

RBC Bearings, Inc.

RBC designs and manufactures precision bearings and engineered components for aerospace, defense, and industrial markets. The stock outperformed in 4Q25 as aerospace demand remained resilient, defense orders accelerated, and operating leverage drove margin expansion. Investors reacted positively to strong free-cash-flow conversion and continued successful integration of prior acquisitions. RBC’s exposure to long-cycle aerospace programs and mission-critical components supported earnings durability.

Repligen Corp.

RGEN supplies bioprocessing equipment and consumables used in biologic drug manufacturing. Shares rebounded strongly as evidence mounted that industry destocking had bottomed and order trends were stabilizing. Improved visibility into 2026 growth, combined with disciplined cost controls, drove operating leverage expectations higher. The market re-rated the stock as investors looked past near-term volatility and focused on RGEN’s high-quality consumables mix, strong market positions, and long-term biologics growth tailwinds.

Digi International, Inc.

DGII provides connectivity solutions, including routers, gateways, and embedded systems. The stock performed well in the quarter as recurring services revenue grew faster than expected and margins expanded with mix shift toward software and subscriptions. Improved demand visibility in industrial and infrastructure end markets, along with disciplined expense management, supported earnings upside. Investors increasingly valued the company’s transition toward a higher-quality, recurring-revenue business model, differentiating it from more hardware-centric small-cap technology peers.

Novanta, Inc.

NOVT provides technology solutions to medical and advanced industrial OEMs, specializing in photonics, vision, and precision motion. The company outperformed in the fourth quarter following a strong earnings report highlighted by double-digit growth in its Advanced Surgery business. Management noted that bookings grew 17% year-over-year, signaling robust future demand as customer inventory levels normalize. We believe NOVT’s ability to exceed profit expectations while navigating a complex macro environment demonstrates the durability of its diversified, high-quality business model.

Small Cap Composite – 4Q25 Bottom 5 Laggards

Stevanato Group SpA

STVN is a global provider of high-performance drug containment and delivery solutions for the pharmaceutical industry. The stock underperformed during the quarter, pressured by two key factors. First, the company’s engineering segment reported a 20% decline in revenue. Although the segment represents only about 15% of total revenue, its turnaround has taken longer than anticipated, weighing on overall results. Second, investor concerns have grown around the potential impact of FDA approval and the introduction of oral GLP-1 therapies. Given STVN’s focus on injectable drug delivery, uncertainty regarding the eventual market share of oral alternatives within the broader GLP-1 category has created near-term uncertainty.

FirstService Corp.

FSV is a leading provider of essential property services to residential and commercial customers in North America. Shares detracted from performance after the company reported third-quarter revenues that fell short of consensus expectations, despite meeting earnings per share estimates. Investors expressed concern over softer organic growth and near-term headwinds in certain service lines. We continue to believe the company’s recurring revenue model and leadership position in fragmented markets remain intact for the long term.

AAON, Inc.

AAON designs and manufactures high-efficiency HVAC systems for commercial and industrial applications. Despite solid 3Q25 results, shares retreated as backward-looking industry channel checks highlighted ERP-related delivery delays and extended lead times. With a relatively new CEO still establishing credibility with the Street, execution concerns—even if transitory—weighed on sentiment given the rapid pace of innovation in the data center cooling market. We believe operational improvements are underway and AAON's strong hyperscaler relationships position the company well for renewed growth.

Construction Partners, Inc.

ROAD is a vertically integrated civil infrastructure company constructing roadways and highways across the Sunbelt. The stock underperformed during the quarter following the release of fiscal fourth-quarter results, where earnings per share missed analyst estimates. While the company delivered better-than-expected revenue growth and highlighted a record backlog, the market focused on the bottom-line shortfall. We remain confident in the company’s ability to benefit from sustained infrastructure demand and its disciplined acquisition strategy.

Trex Co., Inc.

TREX manufactures composite decking and railing products for residential outdoor living markets. Performance was pressured in 4Q25 due to heightened competitive intensity, including increased pricing pressure and share competition from both established and lower-priced alternatives, which weighed on volumes and margins. While the company maintains market leadership and a strong brand, the stock reflected investor concern around rising competition and the sustainability of near-term profitability in a more crowded marketplace. We sold the stock in 4Q25.

Source: FactSet

Small Cap Composite – 4Q25 Buys*

Badger Meter, Inc.

BMI is a leading provider of water meters and connected smart-metering solutions primarily for municipal water utilities. The company exhibits hallmarks of high quality, operating in an oligopolistic water-metering market providing must-have solutions, replacement-driven recurring revenue, healthy capital structure, and good management. Decades of consistent execution, recurring revenues, and attractive free-cash-flow generation supports BMI's ability to grow durably and compound value through economic cycles.

Karman Holdings, Inc

KRMN is a defense technology company specializing in the design and manufacturing of highly engineered, mission-critical systems for a diverse set of existing and next-generation space, missiles, hypersonic, and defense programs. KRMN generates mid-teens or better organic revenue growth that is complimented by tuck-in M&A. They sell to over 70 customers across more than 100 defense programs. Over 90% of revenue is IP protected and nearly 90% of revenue is bought by their customers on a sole source basis.

Small Cap Composite – 4Q25 Sells*

Trex Co., Inc.

TREX manufactures composite decking and railing products for residential outdoor living markets. Performance was pressured in 4Q25 due to heightened competitive intensity, including increased pricing pressure and share competition from both established and lower-priced alternatives, which weighed on volumes and margins. While the company maintains market leadership and a strong brand, the stock reflected investor concern around rising competition and the sustainability of near-term profitability in a more crowded marketplace. We sold the stock in 4Q25.

Mesa Laboratories, Inc.

MLAB provides quality-control instruments, consumables, and services to regulated industries, including life sciences and healthcare. MLAB represented a small position within the small-cap strategy and we lacked sufficient conviction in the company’s long-term growth prospects to increase our exposure. Consequently, we exited the position. The company’s operating results have been pressured by end-market weakness, particularly in cell and gene therapy and in China. In addition, MLAB’s acquisition strategy—especially within clinical genomics— has not delivered the growth we initially anticipated. With limited organic growth opportunities and a balance sheet that constrains further acquisitions, we believe MLAB’s overall growth prospects remain limited.

Conestoga added to positions on three occasions and trimmed stocks on three occasions during the fourth quarter.

*Portfolio holdings shown above experienced material activity during the quarter.

The "Total Assets" include UMA assets and are not part of the GIPS firm assets. UMA and Total Assets are shown as supplemental information.

The current management fee schedule is as follows: Up to $25,000,000 = 1.00%; Over $25,000,000 = Negotiable.

The dispersion of annual returns is measured by the standard deviation across equal-weighted portfolio returns represented within the Composite for the full year. Dispersion is shown as "N/A" for periods less than one year and for periods with 5 or fewer composite members for the entire year.

As of December 31, 2025, the three-year standard deviation, calculated net of fees, for the Conestoga Small Cap Equity Composite was 17.66% and the Russell 2000 Growth was 20.04%. In addition, the weighting of an individual security within a given account cannot exceed 10% (or 2.5 times the target weighting defined in the small capitalization model portfolio) of the equity assets. data.title: Weekly Market Pulse: Central Planning Gone Awry
data.description:
Most investment firms kick off the new year with “outlook” pieces, offering futile predictions on the S&P 500’s year-end close or the frequency of Fed rate hikes. I try to avoid such oracular activity and stick to analyzing the present, which isn’t as easy as it first sounds. But I do read many of these tomes – if brevity is the soul of wit, Wall Street is witless – so I can monitor the consensus view, primarily because it is almost always wrong. Knowing what isn’t going to happen is almost as valuable as knowing what is.

The consensus I gleaned from this year’s crystal ball gazing efforts was that 2026 would be a year of “stability” and falling policy uncertainty. It took only 17 days for that narrative to crumble. On January 17th, President Trump announced a 10% tariff on Denmark and seven other European nations in an escalation of his bid for Greenland.

Trump Announces New European Tariffs in Greenland Standoff; Allies Outraged

New York Times

Other notable signs of stability include:

  • Geopolitical Tensions: Mass demonstrations in Iran with threats of U.S. intervention.
  • Domestic Unrest: Riots in Minneapolis and Chicago following a fatal confrontation between a citizen and ICE.
  • Foreign Interventions: U.S. military action in Venezuela to capture Nicolas Maduro, followed by the seizure of Venezuelan oil production with little apparent long-term planning.

Anyone expecting policy certainty during this administration is on a fool’s errand.

The Myth of “Low Impact” Tariffs

Another common theme in the 2026 outlooks was that businesses could finally “adjust” to the new tariff regime. Bulls argue that the tariffs weren’t that bad because effective rates turned out lower than the announced rates (due to substitution and evasion) and that aggregate data shows little harm. Indeed, through Q3 2025, real GDP growth sat at 2.3%—right in line with the long-term trend. The inflation rate, while still above the Fed’s target, is also right in line with the long-term trend.

However, looking at aggregates obscures the reality of winners and losers. Tariffs generally protect the few at the expense of the many. A few industries or favored companies get the benefits while everyone who uses their products pays the price. Primary metals producers added to payrolls last year, while manufacturing shed jobs in nine of the last twelve months. Since the April tariff announcement, there hasn’t been a single “up” month for manufacturing employment.

The postmortem on the steel and aluminum tariffs from Trump’s first term showed that job losers far outnumbered job gainers. I guess the thinking in his second term is that if a little didn’t work, maybe a lot will, so he raised them and added copper to the bill of particulars.

Contradictory Policy Goals

The administration claims tariffs will revive American manufacturing, but their policies are often self-defeating. Steel, copper and aluminum tariffs imposed for national security reasons make it more expensive to reshore manufacturing and create all those iPhone screw-turning jobs Commerce Secretary Lutnick promised us.

Energy policy is similarly muddled. While seizing Venezuelan oil might temporarily lower gasoline prices, if crude stays below $60 for too long, domestic production in the Permian Basin will stall – and may have already peaked in any case. Improving a consumer’s purchasing power in New England comes at the expense of job losses in West Texas or North Dakota, where drilling has already slowed.

Furthermore, lower shale oil production leads to lower natural gas production. Unlike twenty-five years ago, today’s natural gas prices translate almost immediately into higher electricity bills.

The AI Infrastructure Bottleneck

Electricity prices are skyrocketing due to the AI infrastructure buildout.

This decade’s price surge is exceeded only by the 1970s. Tariffs exacerbate this by raising the cost of copper, aluminum, and steel—the very materials required to build data centers and power plants.

The labor market presents another hurdle. Building the “AI future” requires a massive influx of skilled tradespeople. Electricians, HVAC technicians, plumbers, welders, and carpenters are in high demand right now and all are expected to continue growing rapidly over the next 10 years

Trade Occupation 10-Year Growth Rate 2026 Median Salary (Est.) Primary Growth Driver
Electrician (Nuclear) 14.0% — 18.0% $105,000+ Small Modular Reactors (SMRs) for data centers.
Electrician (Standard) 9.00% $72,500 EV infrastructure and commercial AI builds.
HVAC Technician 8.50% $68,000 Advanced liquid cooling for AI server farms.
Ironworker 6.00% $63,000 Structural steel for massive hyperscale shells.
Plumber / Pipefitter 5.00% $74,000 Industrial piping and high-tech liquid cooling.
Welder 3.0% — 5.0% $61,000 Manufacturing and infrastructure hardening.
Carpenter 1.50% $58,000 Lagging due to shifts toward modular construction.

As demand for these skills rises for data center construction, wages rise, affecting the costs of all other construction. Our inability to build new generating capacity rapidly will likely act as a brake on AI development at some point. Electricity demand flatlined in the 2010s but started to rise after COVID and accelerated in 2024 (3%). Demand from data centers and EVs will likely keep demand growing at that rate – or more – for some time, as long as we can generate the electricity to run it.

The non-data center construction industry was already short-staffed because of AI even before the recent immigration crackdown. Now, a lack of immigrants could mean that entry-level apprentice roles go unfilled and the shortages persist. One wonders how many houses weren’t built last year due to construction labor shortages and how much AI and tariffs added to the cost of the ones that did get built. The administration’s solution to housing affordability—allowing people to use 401(k) funds for down payments—is economically illiterate. Increasing demand in a supply-constrained market will only drive housing prices higher, while using retirement accounts to fund it will only worsen the existing shortfall in retirement savings. If you want to fix housing, you have to work on the supply side.

The Scarcity Age

We are entering an age of scarcity. Our population is aging, prime working age labor participation is near an all-time high, and population growth this year will likely be nil if not outright negative due to current immigration policies. AI is often touted as a solution to labor shortages, but building that AI capability is incredibly resource-intensive and, ironically, requires a lot of skilled workers. A single hyperscale data center requires roughly 50,000 tons of copper and 15,000 tons of steel. Meanwhile, the copper, silver, uranium, platinum and aluminum markets are all already in supply deficit and we’re going to need a lot more. An AI bot requires 10 to 20 times the energy of the human it replaces.

The administration’s political aims are in direct conflict with its economic goals.

  • Immigration policy exacerbates labor shortages in critical trades, raising the cost of data centers and houses alike.
  • Tariff policy raises the cost of raw materials needed to construct our HAL 9000 future
  • Security-driven tariffs undermine the goal of reshoring manufacturing.

This is what F.A. Hayek called “The Fatal Conceit”—the hubris of believing that the government (the President) can predict and control the complex interactions of the global economy. With policy today set seemingly on a whim – how carefully do you think these Greenland tariffs were considered before their announcement on social media? – the potential for a misstep is high. Anyone who believes the next few years will bring more certainty, more predictability, simply isn’t paying attention.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: AppLovin: Thesis Largely Played Out, Downgrade To Sell
data.description:
I was a bull on AppLovin Corporation (APP) since August last year. Since my first strong buy rating, the stock is up 50%, although since my last coverage, the stock has barely found its feet.

I am now downgrading this stock to a sell, as I see compelling evidence that the market has shifted its attention from the near-term growth catalysts (i.e., the move into e-commerce) to other, slightly less exciting developments. Among them, a reported SEC investigation regarding AppLovin’s data collection practices and the underperformance of the advertising agencies industry, where AppLovin is the top stock by market cap.

Overall, I think the market is debating whether the company can defend an 82–83% adjusted EBITDA margin as it pushes deeper into e-commerce, while scrutiny around its data practices persists. At approximately 60x forward earnings, it is my view that the stock leaves little room for any normalization in profitability or adverse headlines.

Unlike Evercore, which recently initiated coverage with an outperform rating, I downgraded mine to a sell, with a price target of $500 as the next technical support. Below, I explain why and how I can be wrong.

Capital Is Flowing Out of The Advertising Sector

Let me first explain AppLovin’s broader problem.

The pressure seems to be from a liquidity perspective. Take a look below at the performance of the different sectors in the S&P 500 for the past month.

Communication services have been lagging behind other sectors. That's unusual, given that this sector was the second in the entire S&P 500 if we look at the 12-month performance.

Now things look even more raw if I zoom into the communication services segment and show the recent performance of its 7 industries.

As you can see above, advertising agencies (AppLovin is the largest company by market cap in this industry) have been the main drag on the communication services sector, especially over the past month.

There’s no doubt that sentiment around this stock is low. In my experience, when a stock (and its industry) is this gloomy, any announcement (no matter the flavor) becomes an excuse for investors to head for the exits. Which brings me to my next point.

A Deteriorating Narrative

Since some sources reported back in October that the Securities and Exchange Commission was investigating AppLovin’s data collection practices after a whistleblower complaint, shares have failed to break new highs.

I believe some worries came from a Bloomberg report stating that the company shut down Array, a product linked to allegations of unwanted app downloads.

Adding to the downward pressure, no other than CEO Adam Foroughi sold shares in multiple transactions between November 20 and 21, followed by the CTO just a few days later.

In my view, the main overhang on the stock is the reported SEC investigation. I think the market may be worried that AppLovin’s edge depended on access to signals controlled by platform partners (i.e., the Apple/Google app ecosystems) and that an enforcement move by a partner could hit performance or growth. Speaking of which, expectations are quite high.

Here is the Street's revenue consensus for the next few quarters:

Just to clarify, the apparent jump in revenue growth from Q2 2026 onward isn’t driven by acquisitions or any catalysts. It is related to last year's decision to sell the mobile gaming business to Tripledot (completed in Q2 2025) for $400M in cash. Since then, the results related to the Apps business have been presented as discontinued operations. So the base quarter (Q2 2025) you’re comparing against in Q2 2026 is already reflecting the streamlined company.

For reference, here is the Street's EPS forecast:

The growth in EPS is where I'm mainly worried. The last 10-Q shows an adjusted EBITDA margin of 82.4%, quite a jump from the 77.5% a year ago.

In the Q3 2025 release, AppLovin guided Q4 2025 to $1.57–$1.60B revenue and $1.29–$1.32B adj. EBITDA. That implies an adjusted EBITDA margin in the 82–83% range.

That's a very decent margin if you ask me. In fact, when comparing EBITDA margin with some of its industry peers, AppLovin stands out as the indisputable leader.

It is my view that a good part of the premium that investors are paying for AppLovin's stock is due to this high margin. Therefore, regardless of the outcome of the SEC investigation, the push into e-commerce (where competition with Meta and Alphabet is likely to be fierce) could pressure the high EBITDA margin.

The main risk that I would monitor this year is the EBITDA guidance (and management's rationale behind the numbers). A normalization could re-rate the stock.

Valuation

AppLovin is not a cheap stock. On a 1-year forward basis, investors are paying 60x for next year's earnings. In comparison, investors are paying today 42x for Trade Desk's next year's earnings.

While AppLovin’s revenue growth over the next 12 months (36%) looks stronger than The Trade Desk’s 16%, I see an increased risk of downward revisions (especially to EPS) if the company can’t sustain its high adjusted EBITDA margin. In my view, it will all depend on the outcomes from the SEC investigation and the push in e-commerce.

How Can I Be Wrong

Even if growth moderates, the company has enough free cash flows to fund buybacks, which mechanically increase earnings per share. Q3 2025 free cash flow was $1.05B. Out of that amount, the company bought 1.3M shares, worth $571 million.

There is more.

The Board increased the company's repurchase authorization by $3.2B in the last quarter, now sitting at $3.3B. That's enough dry powder to compensate for some headwinds in EPS. Overall, I would keep a close eye on any major increase in buybacks over the next few quarters.

If the figure increases significantly (above $1B per quarter), it could be a sign that EPS (ex-buybacks) is under pressure.

However, the biggest pillar of the bulls is the company's move into e-commerce. AppLovin entered the e-commerce space back in 2024 with only a few hundred advertisers under an invite-only program. By Q1 2025, the company said that its e-commerce advertising business reached a $1B run-rate.

More recently, on October 1, 2025, AppLovin launched Axon Ads Manager on an invitation basis, stating that they plan to launch more broadly in H1 2026. In the last earnings call, management provided a very encouraging update:

While it takes a while for new customers to get going, to integrate, to learn how to use our system, and to ramp spend, we're already seeing spend from these self-service advertisers grow around roughly 50% week-over-week. It's too soon to be significant, but this type of early growth gives us even more confidence that our platform will excel at being an open platform to any type of advertiser.

This e-commerce expansion is one of the factors that Evercore analysts cited when they initiated their outperform rating on this stock earlier this year.

There is no doubt that the growth in e-commerce could cast a shadow over many of the current worries. So, why am I bearish?

What To Monitor In Q4 2025 Earnings

One of the key items will be the adjusted EBITDA margin. I would focus on whether management can defend the 82–83% range while scaling further into e-commerce. Therefore, the guidance for Q1 2026 will be key.

Other items include any comments on the Axon Ads Manager, especially if the company provides a concrete revenue figure for the e-commerce segment. Finally, I would monitor buybacks. Anything above $1B a quarter could be a signal of EPS pressure.

Conclusion

To be direct, my sell rating is mainly related to the poor sentiment around risk assets in the US. Advertising agencies are dragging down the performance of the communication services sector, and AppLovin is the top stock by market cap in this industry.

I need to see an improvement in sentiment, and I don't see an immediate near-term catalyst. In fact, the reported SEC investigation could bring a downside catalyst. On top of that, I believe e-commerce is a tough market (given the competition from Google, Meta, and TikTok), so I doubt whether the company can sustain its high adjusted EBITDA margins. Any normalization could lead to a re-rate, given the high forward P/E multiple.

Overall, I believe shares are likely to re-test the $500s and consolidate around this level. I downgrade my rating to a sell, and I recommend monitoring the next earnings release for any guidance on margins and the e-commerce segment.

data.title: FIAX: A Hedge-Fund Like Structure Which Under-Delivers
data.description:
Thesis

The Nicholas Fixed Income Alternative ETF (FIAX) is a fund we started covering a year ago, when we went into detail in respect to the fund structure and composition. At the time we stated the following:

A retail investor, however, needs to understand the ETF is taking equity risk factors in order to generate fixed income returns. The equity risk has capped downside and upside, but could also represent a drag on performance if the market moves against the fund. FIAX represents an alternative in the space as long as the managers deliver on the market risk taken.

In today's article we are going to revisit the fund in light of its 2025 performance and risk factors and outline our view for the fund in the new macro paradigm.

What does the fund do, and what does it hold

Let us first revisit the fund objective so investors have a refreshed view on that:

The Nicholas Fixed Income Alternative ETF Fund is an actively managed exchange-traded fund (“ETF”) that seeks to provide income using U.S. Treasury fixed income securities and a “defined risk option premium.”

The Fund’s defined risk option premium strategy uses options on ETFs across multiple asset classes (e.g., equities, commodities, fixed income). Through the defined risk option premium strategy, two options transactions are paired together in order to create a “defined risk” trade that caps the maximum possible gains and losses from the outset.

So FIAX works like a mini hedge fund that parks most of its cash in treasuries, while taking capped market risk via option structures. We have seen this structure before in the Simplify Enhanced Income ETF (HIGH), which we cover on the Seeking Alpha platform.

These sorts of funds ultimately rely on the portfolio managers' acumen in order to trade good risk. We called FIAX a mini hedge fund because its risk factors can change every day as the managers see fit. In our last article, we outlined a long semiconductor position the fund was taking via call spreads. Right now, for example, the ETF is long utilities via a call spread option structure:

The XLU ticker is the State Street® Utilities Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLU), and the fund is long the 38 strike call and short the 45 strike call in the amount of 1600 contracts. The expiry date for both legs is March 20, 2026. If XLU does not rally into the respective range at the expiry, the fund loses the paid premium. If the fund does rally into the range, then the structure can make money.

The takeaway for an investor here is that track record matters for FIAX because its risk factors are not unilateral, changing basically every day as managers see fit. The only reliable factors here are represented by performance and analytics, factors that we are going to analyze next.

Disappointing performance in the past year

If we look at what FIAX did in the past year, we can observe a pattern:

Just like its brethren, Simplify Enhanced Income ETF, FIAX got burned in the risk-off market from April 2025, and never got back on its feet. In fact, the total return for FIAX is converging with HIGH, which is disappointing for both names.

The ETF takes proprietary market positions (albeit with capped risk), and thus relies on the market factors moving in the desired direction in order to make money. It seems the managers never put on significant correct risk after the April/May 2025 event, thus never even managed to outperform simple cash in 2025.

The only way to measure a fund like FIAX is through actual performance and analytics. The name cycled through many long and short positions via options, but did not manage to create positive results. In fact, the options positions created a negative total return, since the fund overall produced a result inferior to just holding treasuries outright.

Analytics

Despite its poor performance, the fund managed to maintain a tight volatility profile:

The ETF has a low standard deviation of 3.9%, and a low annualized volatility of 4.17%. This is due to the usage of call and put spreads, structures that are intrinsically capped in terms of gains and losses.

While a low volatility profile is good, one does need to produce high total returns in order to obtain a high Sharpe ratio. It is not the case here, with FIAX clocking in a Sharpe of -0.37. A negative Sharpe ratio means that an investment’s return was lower than the risk-free rate; thus, an investor would have been better off keeping their money in a government bond.

So with FIAX, we are looking at a low risk and very low return type of investment. The main risk factor here is the ability of the portfolio managers to put winning positions on the books, thus the hedge fund-like nature of this ETF. Unfortunately, the name has done very poorly, and the cost of opportunity is very high in today's market. For us there is no reason to underwrite this name and the managers when there are many other opportunities out there to generate significant yields. The most we could tolerate would be a total return in line with outright cash, but it is not the case here. The fund actually subtracted from a pure cash position via the options trades.

Conclusion

FIAX is an exchange-traded fund from the XFunds family. The name runs a core treasuries position and layers options spreads on top similarly to a hedge fund. FIAX has not been able to generate a positive return in the past year from its options positions, although it has kept its volatility profile fairly low. With a negative Sharpe ratio and underwhelming results, we see no reason to keep capital allocated here.

data.title: SkyWater Technology: One Year Later, The Easy Money Is Gone
data.description:
When I wrote about SkyWater Technology, Inc. (SKYT) last January, the appeal was pretty simple. The business was strategically important, very early in its scaling journey, and priced like that was its final form, so to speak. I think the market was looking at its choppy Advanced Technology Services (ATS) revenue and uneven profitability while not fully recognizing the long-term value of a trusted, domestic foundry model running with customer-funded capex.

It’s a year later now, and SKYT has more than doubled from the top end of the valuation range I was comfortable with at the time. That move has come from the market treating it like the strategic manufacturing asset that it really is, and I believe that it is now priced just right, so my posture has shifted to a Hold. In this piece, I want to look at the company’s performance with integration, execution, and delivery at a larger scale.

What Has Changed Over The Last Year?

SkyWater is still the same kind of business as it was 12 months ago, but it is much bigger and more consistent. It is a US-based specialty foundry that sits away from leading-edge nodes and focuses instead on trusted manufacturing, co-development, and long-cycle programs. The mix of wafer services and advanced technology services is still central to its business, and most of the demand still comes from customers who care more about security, reliability, and domestic supply than bleeding-edge performance.

I’d divide 2025 into two halves when discussing the company because of its Fab 25 acquisition. I’ll also refer to its business segments as Legacy SkyWater and SkyWater Texas, as management did in the Q3 2025 earnings report. The former didn’t perform particularly well in the first half of the year: revenue from its Tools section fell pretty sharply compared to 2024’s numbers, and ATS spending from aerospace and defense was very uneven because of contract timing issues.

I will say that I think the acquisition is the single most important thing it did last year because before that, it was essentially a one-fab company in Minnesota built around a mix of ATS and specialty wafer manufacturing. After buying the 200mm wafer Fab 25 facility in Austin, Texas, for a total of $93 million from Infineon Technologies in June 2025, the numbers changed pretty dramatically. The acquisition came with an anchor customer and a multi-year wafer supply agreement that includes minimum volume commitments. When I was reviewing the earnings materials from the last few quarters, the one thing that really jumped out was how much the fab changed SKYT’s trajectory, and we’ve seen it in the results.

In Q3 FY25, the Texas facility generated $86.6 million of wafer services revenue, way more than the entire legacy business produced in any of the preceding three quarters. So, the company is now running with manufacturing-led revenue, and that gives it more visibility into utilization rates so it can plan better. Additionally, SKYT doesn’t have to rely solely on ATS timing to deliver strong quarterly results, so the operating model is now more complex and scalable. That distinction is crucial for how I see the company’s execution risk and valuation moving forward.

Of course, ATS is still a key part of the story, and it is doing its part by pulling the company into programs early. Customer-funded tooling still limits how much capital SKYT can risk upfront, too, but everything is now supporting a much bigger footprint. I like that.

The Numbers Don’t Lie

When I step back and look at the financials since my original piece, the most important thing to understand is that SkyWater’s growth over the last year is real, but it is also uneven by design. The headline change is scale. In Q3 FY25, the company’s total consolidated revenue reached $150.7 million, up 61% year over year, and that jump is almost entirely because SkyWater Texas came online and contributed $86.6 million of wafer services revenue in its first full quarter as part of the company.

On the other hand, Legacy SkyWater only generated $64.1 million, only slightly better than the previous two quarters’ average of $60 million. Still, it was down over 30% year over year, which explains why the surface-level growth story looks confusing if you don’t split the segments out.

Q3 FY25 Revenue

Amount (USD mm)

Legacy SkyWater (ATS + Wafer)

$60.4

Tools revenue

$3.7

Total Legacy SkyWater

$64.1

SkyWater Texas (Wafer Services)

$86.6

Total Revenue

$150.7

Now, I want to discuss how the legacy business’s own segments performed. ATS development revenue came in at $54.2 million, slightly higher than in Q2 but still lower on a YoY basis. That decline is not a demand issue, and management pulled roughly $4 million of ATS revenue forward into Q3 because of aerospace and defense program timing. Meanwhile, the company was also contending with some delays in broader government funding approvals. At the same time, the revenue from wafer services in Minnesota actually grew sequentially, but it’s still a small piece of the legacy mix.

If we look at Tools revenue, its performance wasn’t surprising either. It collapsed year over year, falling 88% from $30.7 million in Q3 FY24 to $3.7 million in Q3 FY25. This was mostly because the segment’s sales depend entirely on customer procurement decisions and don’t repeat cleanly. It was pretty much the same story in Q1 and Q2.

With margins, the story is a bit more interesting. Non-GAAP gross margin rose to 24.6% in Q3, up more than 500 basis points sequentially, and that boost came from a combination of higher Texas wafer volumes, favorable purchase accounting effects, and about $5 million in non-recurring cost reversals. Obviously, some of those benefits are a one-time thing, so we cannot fully attribute the margin improvement to the business’s performance. That’s fine, but it also showed how much operating leverage exists when utilization improves. Besides that, adjusted EBITDA reached $25.8 million (17.1% of revenue), way, way above expectations.

The Value Setup Is Different Now

SkyWater’s current valuation is far removed from where it was when I called a Buy, and justifiably so. Back then, the stock was cheap because the market didn’t believe scale would show up, at least not that quickly. Today, that scale is here, and the stock is priced accordingly.

Let us consider things on a revenue basis, normalizing Q3 FY25 as a starting point and thinking about FY26 as the first full year with Texas. That would bring FY26 revenues well over $600 million, and using the current share base of roughly 48.5 million shares outstanding, we get revenue per share around $12.5. That’s a big jump from last January, when that ratio was closer to $7 with roughly the same share count, so clearly, the underlying equity value is a lot stronger.

Today, the stock is also trading at ~3.7x forward P/S, well above the re-rating I argued for last year, too. With EBITDA, the story is a bit different. Yes, SKYT’s adjusted EBITDA in Q3 FY25 was strong on paper, but you must keep in mind that it benefited from a few one-off benefits too. Right now, its EV/EBITDA multiple is more than double the sector median of ~16x, which is a pretty hefty premium for a company that is just starting to scale revenue, and we need to see that performance repeated consistently. You might not think it’s expensive, in isolation, but at these levels and compared to the general industry multiples, it is difficult to argue that the stock hasn’t outrun the business at this stage.

Finally, we can’t ignore dilution. I mentioned the share count earlier, and it has crept up very modestly over the past year, mostly because of equity compensation. SKYT hasn’t used its ATM much during FY25, which is good, but again, the reality is that the per-share math now assumes execution at a larger scale. The market isn’t paying these prices for a company that may still struggle with operational hiccups or unstable margins–execution is the driver here–and I can’t see any margin of safety at the current price.

What I’m Watching In Q4: Catalysts

Q4 is the first quarter where SkyWater is guiding the business with Fab 25 fully in the picture, and the targets are pretty clear. You can find them below.

Here’s what I’m looking out for when those Q4 results come out in a few weeks.

I’m watching whether Texas holds the line at high-$80M-ish revenue without needing any surprise WIP dynamics to make the quarter look good. In the Q3 deck, management explained that Texas beat expectations partly because of a higher-than-forecast off-market component and additional revenue from work completed on the elevated WIP immediately post-acquisition. That’s fine, but it also means that Q4 is where the run-rate starts to matter more. We can't have one-off benefits clouding the true picture of things.

I’m also keeping an eye on Tools. The segment has been unstable for a hot minute, and management is guiding $17M–$18M in Q4 tools revenue. During the Q3 FY25 earnings call, they noted that one tool shifts to 2026, which tells me the lumpiness hasn’t gone anywhere. Some stability would be great, and it is crucial for me to be able to assign a value to Tools as a major contributor moving forward.

And then there’s margins. Q3 non-GAAP gross margin printed at 24.6%, way above what they guided, but Q4 guidance (17%–20% non-GAAP GM) is basically them telling us not to anchor to Q3 as the new normal. It’s even more poignant when you remember that Q4 still benefits from some carryover, so we might be dealing with a lower figure when everything normalizes. I want to see that number averaging around the 20, 21% mark before I can put any weighting to it as a catalyst.

I’m Bullish On The Business But Not Enough To Bite At This Price

I’ve laid out why I’m not convinced that $33/share is a good price to buy into SKYT, even though the fundamentals are much better now than they were a year ago. I've also listed the things I need to see over a sustained period to get off the fence--I’m talking financially and operationally. For me, Q4 is a proof quarter, and even if the company achieves what it laid out in guidance, I’d still be waiting for consistent performance over a few quarters before reassigning a Buy to the stock. I believe that at this level, patience is more important than chasing a hot stock.

data.title: Parnassus Value Select ETF Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The strategy seeks to achieve strong long-term returns through a high-conviction, highly concentrated portfolio of approximately 25 undervalued U.S. large-cap stocks that are poised to rise but are temporarily out of favor relative to their history or peers.

Market Review

The Russell 1000 Value Index ended a strong year with solid gains in the fourth quarter and double-digit returns for the year as investors continued to focus on the ongoing megatrend in AI. This investment theme continues to surprise to the upside, demonstrating that it is likely here to stay. The Federal Reserve (Fed) cut interest rates for the third time in 2025, against the backdrop of a relatively steady economy with modest GDP growth, a relatively strong labor market and moderating inflation, although the benefits weren’t felt equally across the economy. These stable economic conditions and strong earnings growth also helped to boost markets. During the quarter, the best-performing sectors in the Russell 1000 Value Index were Information Technology, Communication Services and Health Care, while the Real Estate and Utilities sectors ended the quarter with slight losses. The strong returns across a range of sectors reflected the broadening of the AI theme, not just the continuation of it.

Quarterly Performance Review

Broadening sector strength boosted relative performance

The Fund returned 5.85% (NAV) for the quarter, outperforming the Russell 1000 Value Index’s 3.81%. The outperformance was notably driven by our Utilities and Industrials holdings and an Information Technology overweight, fueled by positive sentiment toward the latest AI developments. The relative gains were partially offset by holdings in the Materials and Consumer Discretionary sectors.

Top Quarterly Contributors

Micron Technology (MU) shares outperformed due to the company's continued strong position in the AI-driven memory market. Management noted a robust demand environment for its HBM DRAM chips.

Applied Materials (AMAT) shares climbed amid the accelerating adoption of AI and the subsequent demand for advanced semiconductors and wafer fab equipment.

AstraZeneca (AZN) advanced due to a combination of positive clinical trial results for key drugs and significant expansion of its U.S. manufacturing footprint.

Cummins (CMI) rallied after the engines and industrial equipment maker reported strong third-quarter sales results due to strong growth in the distribution and power systems segment driven by data center demand.

Alphabet (GOOGL) posted strong performance in the quarter as resilient revenues for its search business, improving growth in its cloud segment and renewed confidence in its vertically integrated AI strategy lifted investor sentiment.

NICE (NICE), a software company providing contact center solutions, lost investor confidence due to concerns over AI disruption. However, we believe that the company should see AI transform its business model and drive long-term growth.

Home Depot (HD) shares declined due to continued pressure from housing market weakness and lower confidence in consumer spending. However, the company remains well positioned to capture upside from a potential recovery in the home improvement market.

Verizon Communications (VZ) shares detracted after the surprise replacement of CEO Hans Vestberg with former PayPal CEO Dan Schulman in early October. Investors also turned away from defensive stocks during the quarter.

Portfolio Positioning

Portfolio anchored on quality amid elevated market valuations

We continue to maintain a balanced, quality-anchored positioning in the portfolio. Our bottom-up fundamental assessment drives this risk-aware exposure across industries that we believe will benefit from long-term thematic tailwinds. We also maintain ample exposure to defensive holdings that will preserve capital should markets experience increased volatility or a pullback in the coming quarters.

The Fund's largest sector overweights are in Information Technology, Health Care and Communications Services. Our three largest underweights are in Energy (no exposure), Consumer Staples and Consumer Discretionary.

Our overweight in Information Technology was modestly lower versus the third quarter as we continue to realize gains in select long-term secular winners. While we remain constructive on the sector’s structural growth drivers, we are also cognizant of valuation levels, particularly as market gains have broadened across a wider array of beneficiaries in recent quarters.

During the quarter, we modestly increased our overweight in the Health Care sector with the addition of Abbott Laboratories (ABT). We like Abbott’s leadership position in diabetes care, heart health and electrophysiology, all supported by ongoing innovation and new product launches. Overall, we like the attractive risk/reward opportunity in Health Care, especially as the sector emerges from several headwinds faced over the past three years.

We reduced our overweight in the Communications Services sector with the exit of Comcast. We divested from Comcast due to a deterioration in our investment thesis as cable companies face rising competition from wireless providers.

Outlook

Market environment favors continued outperformance for value stocks

Value stocks outperformed growth stocks in the fourth quarter as the Russell 1000 Value index rose 3.81% compared to a 1.12% rise in the Russell 1000 Growth index. We expect the current market environment to continue favoring value stocks. High market valuations for growth stocks, a relatively benign economic backdrop, potential for lower rates and stimulative policy levers are all supportive of attractive risk/reward potential in value stocks.

Moreover, we believe the once-in-a-generation AI investments being made today will, over time, drive productivity gains, business model innovation and earnings growth across a much broader range of industries than is being appreciated by investors today. AI has the potential to impact every sector, not just technology, similar to earlier waves of automation and technology adoption. The timing of these benefits admittedly remains uncertain, which underpins the Fund's diversified exposure across sectors and across time—some will benefit this year, some over three years or longer. However, what we believe is more certain is that regardless of the sector, companies that have a strong track record of investing in innovation and the scale to justify these upfront investments will eventually emerge stronger through AI. This was true in prior innovation cycles, and this time will be no different.

The high-quality companies that we own in the Value Select ETF—across Banking, Payments, Pharma, Medical Devices or Agricultural Equipment, among others—all possess the scale to invest ahead of peers and a demonstrated ability to translate investments into enduring shareholder value.

Our time-tested investment process with a disciplined focus on both risk and return has served us well over the last two decades. We remain confident that the portfolio is well positioned to navigate both macroeconomic and thematic crosscurrents in the coming years. We will continue to focus on identifying quality companies trading at attractive relative valuations in the pursuit of strong long-term returns.
